profile.behavior: Ground forager that runs or pauses upright while searching for invertebrates; often flicks its tail after landing. Feeds on fruit as well as animal prey, and may gather in flocks around berry-bearing trees outside the breeding season.
field_marks: Large, round-bodied thrush with long legs and a fairly long tail
Warm rusty-orange breast and belly, usually contrasting with darker upperparts
Dark gray to blackish head; females and immatures are generally paler
Yellow bill, often with a darker tip
Pale eye arcs or broken white eye ring; white lower belly and undertail coverts can show clearly
profile.habitat: Open woodlands and forest edges, shrublands, fields and pastures, and human-altered green spaces such as gardens and parks; uses trees or shrubs for nesting and fruiting vegetation for seasonal food.

sources.title: American Robin Identification — Cornell Lab of Ornithology

American Robin — U.S. National Park Service

findings: Independent verification confirms the common name, scientific name, family, all three measurement ranges, and the stated core field marks. The plate correctly depicts an adult male American Robin: round-bodied thrush proportions; dark head; broken pale eye arcs; yellow bill with darker tip; rusty-orange underparts; slate-gray wings; white lower belly/undertail; long legs; and a fairly long, rounded tail.

The anatomy is coherent: one head, one beak, two legs, one tail, and a normal paired-wing anatomy are represented without malformed or extra body parts. Feet and toes are somewhat stylized but remain plausible for a passerine.

Visible factual claims match the verified sources, including ground-foraging behavior, upright pauses, tail flicking, berry feeding/flocking, and use of wooded, open, and human-altered habitats. No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.

Minor non-material limitation: the side-view main specimen visibly shows only the near wing; the separate lower wing study usefully documents wing structure but does not show a second attached wing. The vertical centimeter ruler is decorative/illustrative rather than a clearly stated specimen scale.
